Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty flexible and maybe not as structured as it should have been. I'd usually set aside chunks of time during the week, aiming for about 20-something hours total, but I preferred listening to recorded lectures and podcasts while doing other things. Being 26 and having various commitments, I found that audio-based learning fit my lifestyle better. I'd often study in the evenings or during commutes, absorbing content through my headphones rather than sitting at a desk.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I tried to keep some consistency, but I'd be lying if I said I stuck to a rigid schedule. When deadlines approached, I'd ramp up significantly, but during quieter weeks, I might spread things out more casually. My extracurricular commitments sometimes threw off my rhythm too.
Q3: Usually what got me to log into the LMS was checking if there were new announcements or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd go in specifically to download lecture recordings so I could listen to them later offline. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—I needed a specific reason, like an upcoming deadline or wanting to check discussion posts from my peer support group.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS became a daily habit for me, but there were stretches where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the peer support program was active and we were posting in discussions. That social element gave me a reason to return more often. Once I got into a rhythm of downloading materials at the start of each week, that became somewhat automatic, though my actual login frequency stayed pretty low.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I loved being able to listen to them multiple times, rewinding parts I didn't catch the first time. The discussion forums were also valuable because connecting with peers helped me process ideas out loud, so to speak. When someone explained a concept differently in the forums, it often clicked better than reading it in text.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped over the reading materials and text-heavy resources. They just didn't stick for me the way audio content did. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional quizzes because they felt like busywork when I was already confident about the material. If something didn't seem immediately relevant to assignments or exams, I'd probably let it slide.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I needed to submit or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. I also valued anything that involved interaction with other students because those conversations made the learning feel more real. If I could see the practical application or knew it would show up on an exam, I was much more motivated to engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I rarely got around to, honestly. Required activities had built-in urgency that made me prioritize them, while optional ones always got pushed to the back burner. I kept telling myself I'd get to them eventually, but with my motivation being what it was, that rarely happened. There's something about knowing something counts toward your grade that makes it feel more legitimate.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline with downloaded audio files and my own notes. I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back, which wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos related to course topics. My actual learning was way more extensive than what any platform could track.
Q10: There were many times when I'd download a week's worth of lectures and then spend hours listening to them during walks or while cooking, none of which showed up as engagement. I'd also have long phone calls with classmates from my peer support group where we'd talk through difficult concepts. Those conversations were incredibly valuable for my learning, but the LMS had no way of knowing they happened.
Q11: That was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, grab the audio files and any essential documents, then work with them completely offline for days. I might not log back in until the next batch of materials was released or an assignment was due. My watch time on videos in the system was probably way lower than my actual engagement with the content because I preferred the downloaded versions.
Q12: There were definitely weeks when my motivation dipped and everything felt harder. Sometimes it was just general life stuff piling up, and other times I felt disconnected from the course content. My attendance being around 50% reflects those periods when showing up—whether virtually or in person—just felt like too much. I'm generally happy, but low motivation made sustained engagement challenging.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were probably the biggest factor affecting my LMS usage. Being 26, I have more going on than a typical student, and sometimes the course had to take a backseat. Fatigue definitely played a role too—after a long day, logging into yet another platform felt exhausting even when I knew I should. I'd often opt for passive listening over active platform engagement when I was drained.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a lecture playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd technically be logged in and the video would be running, but I wasn't absorbing anything meaningful. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. I'd usually have to revisit that content later when I could properly focus.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on discussion posts and some written assignments even though I knew they mattered. Part of it was low motivation, and part was that writing doesn't come as naturally to me as verbal processing does. I'd put off readings especially, always thinking I'd get to them but often running out of time. It's frustrating because I knew better, but knowing and doing are different things.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly become very active, downloading materials, checking requirements, and submitting work. Without that external pressure, I'd drift along at a much slower pace. My engagement patterns probably looked very spiky—quiet periods followed by bursts of activity right before things were due.
Q17: Feedback had a moderate effect on me. When I got good grades back, it validated that my approach was working even if it wasn't textbook perfect. Constructive criticism would sometimes motivate me to adjust, but honestly, with my low resilience, negative feedback could also make me want to disengage for a bit. The peer support helped buffer some of that because classmates would encourage each other after tough feedback.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could adapt it to my auditory learning preferences and busy schedule. Bad because without rigid requirements, my low motivation meant I'd skip things I probably shouldn't have. Having five online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this course's structure let me get away with doing the minimum sometimes.
Q19: Having recorded lectures available was huge for making engagement easier—that matched exactly how I learn best. The discussion forums connected to peer support also helped because they gave me a social reason to participate. What made things harder was when content was only available in text format or when the platform required specific browser settings. Any friction in accessing materials made me less likely to bother.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are searching for something specific or trying to figure out how the platform works without actually completing tasks. Maybe they're also anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Or they could be like me sometimes—logged in and technically active but not meaningfully engaged because their mind is elsewhere. Platform activity doesn't equal productive work.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was low, but I earned a Merit because my real learning happened offline through audio materials and peer conversations. I think students who know their learning style can be efficient—getting what they need from the platform quickly and then doing the deep work elsewhere. Low login frequency doesn't mean low effort or poor understanding.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you submitted things and maybe how often you logged in, but they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't capture offline studying, conversations with peers, or the mental processing that happens away from screens. For auditory learners like me, most of my learning happens through listening and discussing, which barely registers in any system. Logs show activity, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, discussing them with others, and connecting them to real-world applications. It's not about how many times I logged in or how long videos played—it's about whether the content is actually sinking in and whether I'm motivated to go deeper. True engagement is internal.
Q24: The LMS would completely miss my peer support interactions outside the platform, my hours of listening to downloaded lectures, and the voice memos I made to process concepts. It wouldn't show the conversations with classmates that helped me understand difficult topics or the genuine happiness I felt when something finally clicked. My actual engagement was much richer than my digital footprint suggests, and I think that's true for a lot of students.
